moveAnnotations.annotation: We're playing the Mieses Opening, a rare and offbeat choice that avoids main-line theory entirely. By controlling e4 and preparing to support the center with moves like e4 later, we set up a flexible, solid setup that emphasizes understanding over memorization. This move is named after Jacques Mieses and appeals to players who enjoy positional maneuvering rather than sharp tactical battles—you're essentially saying 'I'll play chess with you on my own terms.'

Black stakes a claim in the center immediately, which is the principled response to our quiet d3. By playing e5, Black seizes space and prevents us from easily establishing our own pawn on e4, forcing us to adapt our plans. This is exactly what we should expect—your opponent won't be intimidated by the slow Mieses, so be prepared to demonstrate that solid, understated moves can lead to a comfortable position if you know what you're doing.
